title: Lower Bounds for Circuits with $MOD_m$ Gates - Part II
speaker: Arkadev Chattopadhyay

proof_stages.label: Exact polynomial representation over Z_m
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up Boolean functions into Z_m as a module, uses delta functions to show every function has a unique multilinear polynomial representation, and defines exact degree as the degree of that representation.

proof_stages.label: Relaxed representation notions
proof_stages.summary: Exact representation is generalized to one-sided, generalized, and weak generalized representations using accepting sets, with emphasis on the loss of uniqueness and the no-false-positives character of weak representations.

proof_stages.label: CRT and composite-modulus degree landscape
proof_stages.summary: Chinese remaindering decomposes polynomials over composite Z_m into prime-modulus components; over primes or prime powers generalized degree is comparable to exact degree, while for composite moduli the Barrington-Beigel-Rudich and Tardos-Barrington results for OR show a large gap and motivate clustering arguments.

proof_stages.label: Weak degree lower bounds for MOD_q
proof_stages.summary: Green’s Fourier-analytic lower bound for weak one-sided representations of MOD_q is combined with the Tardos-Barrington combinatorial method to obtain weak generalized degree lower bounds for MOD_q over Z_m when the moduli are coprime.

proof_stages.label: Single-output MOD gate reduction
proof_stages.summary: For an AC0 circuit feeding a single MOD_m gate, random restrictions plus the switching lemma turn the AC0 subcircuits into small decision trees, and accepting paths yield low-degree polynomials giving a one-sided representation of the restricted circuit.

proof_stages.label: Configuration expansion for many MOD_m gates
proof_stages.summary: For s MOD_m gates ordered topologically, the proof expands over all possible output configurations alpha, replaces guessed MOD values by constants, adds verification circuits, and applies the switching lemma simultaneously to the enlarged AC0 family.

proof_stages.label: Extraction of a weak representation
proof_stages.summary: After restriction, low-degree verification polynomials are selected via a maximal subset of simultaneously active MOD gates, and their joint nonvanishing is shown to imply either f_rho=1 or its complement, allowing them to be merged into a single weak generalized representation.

proof_stages.label: Parameter tradeoff and barrier
proof_stages.summary: The final reduction tracks degree blowup from merging s polynomials and size blowup from 2^s configurations, explaining how stronger weak generalized degree lower bounds would improve circuit lower bounds and why generalized-degree lower bounds are tied to Smolensky-type barriers.

examples.name: Non-coprime MOD case
examples.note: When m divides q, the MOD_q function has an easy linear representation over Z_m, illustrating why the lower-bound statements require the moduli to be coprime.
examples.engagement_reason: for the non-coprime case he analyzes the subcase m divides q and explains how a linear representation is obtained by repeating inputs r times

passes.reason: When the moduli share/divide, the symmetric MOD target collapses to a native linear representation, refuting any coprime-free lower-bound claim.
When the moduli share or divide factors, the MOD target remains permutation-symmetric but collapses to a minimal linear representation, showing the coprime lower-bound condition would fail.
When the MOD moduli are non-coprime, especially m divides q, the modular mismatch collapses to a degree-one symmetric MOD representation, showing coprimality is necessary.
In the divisible non-coprime MOD case the symmetric counting function collapses to a linear representation, showing the coprimality lower-bound condition cannot simply be dropped.
The discussed non-coprime m|q MOD case is permutation-symmetric but collapses to a minimum-degree linear representation, showing the coprime lower bound would fail.
When the moduli are not coprime, the MOD case collapses to an easy low-degree representation, showing the coprime hypothesis is necessary.
The non-coprime MOD setting, especially when one modulus divides the other, is a collapsed modular case where the coprime lower-bound phenomenon fails via an easy linear representation.
When the moduli are not coprime, the MOD function collapses to an easy linear representation, blocking extension of the coprime lower bound.
